Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. At 29, I've got a lot going on outside of school, so I'd just fit studying in whenever I could. Sometimes that meant late nights after everything else was done, sometimes it meant cramming on weekends. I'm more of a hands-on learner anyway, so I'd rather do something practical than sit and read for hours. I'd estimate maybe 22 hours a week, but it was scattered and unplanned.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. I'd go days without touching course material, then suddenly realize something was coming up and put in a burst of effort. Deadlines were really the only thing that forced any kind of structure. I know it's not the best approach, but it's how I've always operated.
Q3: Usually a deadline or an assignment reminder would get me to log in. Sometimes I'd get a notification about discussion posts and feel guilty enough to check in. I wasn't one of those students who logged in daily just to browse around. If there wasn't something specific I needed to do, I probably wasn't logging in.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became part of my normal routine. I'd use it when I had to, but it always felt like a chore rather than a habit. I think the gamified tasks they introduced helped a bit because they made things slightly more interesting, but even then, I wouldn't say I was regularly checking in. It just wasn't something I thought about naturally.
Q5: The video content was useful when I actually watched it, even though I didn't watch much. I learn better by doing, so any hands-on activities or practical examples were what stuck with me. The discussion forums were surprisingly helpful too because I could see how other people approached problems. Reading through those peer interactions sometimes clarified things better than the lectures did.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the reading materials and supplementary PDFs. They just felt too passive for how I learn. I'd download them thinking I'd get to them later, but they'd just sit on my laptop untouched. The lengthy video lectures were another thing I'd skip through or avoid entirely. If something felt like busywork without clear payoff, I probably didn't bother with it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate relevance to the assignments or exams. If I could apply something right away or if it helped me understand a concept I was struggling with, then I'd engage with it. The gamified tasks actually worked on me sometimes because they broke things into smaller, more manageable pieces. But if something felt abstract or disconnected from what I needed to accomplish, I'd tune out.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt completely different. Required ones I'd do because I had to, even if reluctantly. Optional ones? I'd look at them and think 'maybe later' and then never get back to them. There was no real consequence for skipping them, so my motivation to engage was basically zero. I know that's not great, but when you're juggling life and school, you prioritize what's mandatory.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download materials and study them offline all the time. I'd print out notes, use textbooks, and even watch YouTube videos to supplement what the course offered. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS entirely. I'd work through problems on paper, practice things hands-on, and only go back to the LMS when I needed to submit something.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was genuinely studying but the LMS wouldn't show it. I'd have downloaded lecture slides and be working through them at a coffee shop with no internet. Or I'd be doing practice problems from a textbook that had nothing to do with the platform. My exam scores were good because I was putting in work, just not in ways the system could track.
Q11: All the time. I'd log in once, download everything I needed, and then work through it offline over the next several days. My login frequency was low, but that doesn't mean I wasn't engaging with the content. I just preferred having everything saved locally where I could access it without dealing with the platform. One download could fuel a week's worth of studying.
Q12: There were definitely periods where I felt disconnected from the course. Sometimes life stuff would pile up and school became an afterthought. I'd feel this kind of sadness or heaviness that made it hard to care about assignments. During those times, I'd do the bare minimum just to get by. The gamified tasks helped pull me back in a bit, but the underlying disengagement was still there.
Q13: Yeah, all of those things affected me at different points. Fatigue was a big one because I'd come home exhausted and the last thing I wanted to do was log into the LMS. Competing responsibilities definitely took priority over coursework sometimes. When I was confused about something, I'd often just avoid it rather than ask for help. It wasn't the best coping strategy, but it's what I did.
Q14: Definitely. There were times I'd open materials and just stare at them without really absorbing anything. My mind would be elsewhere, thinking about work or personal stuff. I'd technically be logged in and have a video playing, but I wasn't actually learning. Those sessions felt hollow, like I was going through the motions without any real engagement.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. Part of it was procrastination, part of it was feeling overwhelmed by everything else in my life. I'd tell myself I'd get to it tomorrow, and tomorrow would become next week. My assignment completion rate suffered because of this, even though when I did submit work, it was usually good quality.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. I'd see a due date approaching and suddenly kick into gear. Without that external pressure, I'd drift and let things slide. The LMS became this place I'd visit in panic mode right before something was due. It wasn't healthy, but the deadline-driven approach is how I survived the course.
Q17: Feedback did affect me, but in complicated ways. Good feedback made me feel like maybe I could actually do this, which was motivating. But if feedback was delayed or vague, I'd lose interest in that particular area. I appreciated when instructors acknowledged effort because it made me feel seen. The problem was I often didn't check feedback promptly because I wasn't logging in regularly.
Q18: The course structure was fairly flexible, which was both good and bad for someone like me. Good because I could work at my own pace, bad because I need more external structure to stay on track. The gamified elements added some variety that helped break up the monotony. But overall, the structure didn't force me to engage consistently, so I didn't.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement easier because they felt less like traditional coursework. Having clear milestones and some element of competition or achievement helped. What made it harder was when content felt too text-heavy or when there wasn't clear guidance on what was most important. I needed things to be more hands-on and practical to really stay engaged.
Q20: I think those students might be like me in some ways, exploring content without committing to the work. They might be curious but struggling with follow-through. Or maybe they're dealing with personal issues that make it hard to complete assignments even when they're trying to engage. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity, sometimes it's just anxious browsing.
Q21: That actually describes me pretty well. My LMS activity was low, but I still got a distinction because I was learning effectively outside the system. I'd do the work offline, understand the material through my own methods, and then perform well on exams and the assignments I actually submitted. The platform doesn't capture everything that counts as learning.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show understanding or effort put in offline. They miss all the thinking, reading, and practicing that happens away from the computer. They also can't capture emotional engagement or whether someone was genuinely interested versus just going through motions. It's a very incomplete picture.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in effort to understand it. It's not just about showing up or clicking through materials. For me, real engagement happens when I'm hands-on with something, working through problems, and feeling like the content matters. It's a mental and emotional investment, not just a behavioral one.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with motivation or the sadness that made engagement difficult. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent studying offline or the genuine effort I put into understanding material away from the platform. It also wouldn't show that despite appearing disengaged, I actually cared about doing well and worked hard in my own way to achieve that.
